chapter: Chapter 12: Magnetic Effects of Electric Current

questions.question: Define magnetic field and magnetic field lines. List any four properties of magnetic field lines.
questions.answer: Magnetic field is the space around a magnet or current-carrying conductor where magnetic force is experienced by another magnet or moving charge. Magnetic field lines are imaginary lines representing the direction and strength of the magnetic field. Properties: (i) They emerge from north pole and enter south pole externally; (ii) Form closed continuous loops; (iii) Never intersect each other; (iv) Closer lines indicate stronger field; (v) Tangent at any point gives field direction.

questions.question: Why do two magnetic field lines never intersect? Explain with a reason and diagram implication.
questions.answer: If two field lines intersect, it would mean two different directions of magnetic field at the intersection point, which is impossible as magnetic field has a unique direction at every point. Hence, field lines never cross each other.

questions.question: Describe Oersted's experiment to show that electric current produces a magnetic field.
questions.answer: Oersted connected a straight wire to a battery and placed a compass needle parallel to it. When current flowed, the needle deflected perpendicular to the wire. On reversing current, deflection reversed. No deflection when current off. This proved current produces magnetic field around it.

questions.question: State the right-hand thumb rule for magnetic field due to a straight current-carrying conductor. Illustrate with an example.
questions.answer: If right-hand thumb points in direction of current, curled fingers show circular magnetic field lines around conductor. Example: Current upwards → clockwise circles when viewed from above. Field strength decreases with distance (B ∝ 1/r).

questions.question: Draw and explain the magnetic field pattern due to a current-carrying circular loop. Mention direction at centre.
questions.answer: Field lines form closed loops passing through loop. Inside loop, lines nearly parallel and perpendicular to plane. At centre, field strongest and direction given by right-hand rule (thumb along current, fingers curl to show field). Outside, field weaker.

questions.question: Describe the magnetic field produced by a current-carrying solenoid. How does it resemble a bar magnet?
questions.answer: Inside solenoid: uniform, strong, parallel to axis from south to north. Outside: weak, divergent. Ends behave as north and south poles. Resembles bar magnet with field lines emerging from one end and entering the other, forming closed loops.

questions.question: List the factors on which the strength of magnetic field inside a solenoid depends. How can it be increased?
questions.answer: Factors: (i) Current strength (direct proportion); (ii) Number of turns per unit length; (iii) Nature of core (soft iron increases). Increase by: higher current, more turns, soft iron core insertion.

questions.question: State Fleming's left-hand rule. Explain its application in finding force direction in an electric motor.
questions.answer: Fleming's left-hand rule: Forefinger - magnetic field, middle finger - current, thumb - force/motion (mutually perpendicular). In motor, used to determine direction of force on current-carrying coil in magnetic field, causing rotation.

questions.question: Explain the construction and working of an electric motor. Mention the role of split ring commutator.
questions.answer: Construction: Armature coil, strong magnet, split ring commutator, carbon brushes, axle. Working: Current in coil experiences force (Fleming left), rotates coil. Commutator reverses current every half turn, ensuring continuous unidirectional rotation. Converts electrical to mechanical energy.

questions.question: State the principle of electromagnetic induction. Mention Faraday's observations.
questions.answer: Changing magnetic flux through a coil induces emf/current. Faraday showed: Induced emf proportional to rate of flux change. Experiments: Magnet moved in/out coil → galvanometer deflection; faster motion → larger current.

questions.question: Describe an experiment to demonstrate electromagnetic induction using a coil and magnet.
questions.answer: Connect coil to galvanometer. Move bar magnet in/out coil: Deflection observed. Faster movement or stronger magnet → greater deflection. No motion → no current. Proves flux change induces current.

questions.question: State Fleming's right-hand rule and its use in determining induced current direction in a generator.
questions.answer: Fleming's right-hand rule: Thumb - motion, forefinger - field, middle finger - induced current. In generator, used to find direction of induced current when conductor moves in magnetic field.

questions.question: Explain the working of an AC generator with a labelled diagram.
questions.answer: Rectangular coil rotates in uniform magnetic field. Changing flux induces alternating emf (AC). Slip rings and brushes collect output. Direction reverses every half rotation → sinusoidal AC. Mechanical energy to electrical.

questions.question: Differentiate between DC motor and AC generator in terms of energy conversion, commutator, and output.
questions.answer: DC motor: Electrical → mechanical, split ring commutator for unidirectional rotation. AC generator: Mechanical → electrical, slip rings for alternating output. Motor uses current in field for force; generator uses motion in field for emf.

questions.question: Explain Lenz's law with an example. How does it support conservation of energy?
questions.answer: Induced current opposes the change in magnetic flux causing it. Example: Magnet approaching coil → induced current creates field repelling magnet. Opposes motion → work done → energy conserved (mechanical to electrical).

questions.question: What is an electromagnet? How is its strength varied? Give two uses.
questions.answer: Temporary magnet formed by current in solenoid with soft iron core. Strength varied by changing current or turns. Uses: Electric bell, crane for lifting scrap iron, MRI machines.

questions.question: Derive the expression for force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field. When is it maximum?
questions.answer: Force F = B I L sinθ (B - field, I - current, L - length, θ - angle). Maximum when sinθ = 1 (θ = 90°, perpendicular). Direction by Fleming left-hand rule.

questions.question: Explain the causes of overloading and short-circuiting in domestic circuits. How are they prevented?
questions.answer: Overloading: Too many appliances → high current → heating. Short-circuit: Live-neutral contact → very high current. Prevented by fuse (melts) or MCB (trips electromagnetically).

questions.question: Describe the working principle of an electric bell with a diagram explanation.
questions.answer: Electromagnet attracts armature → hammer strikes gong → circuit breaks at contact → electromagnet de-energises → spring returns armature → circuit closes → repeats. Continuous ringing.

questions.question: Why is soft iron preferred as core in electromagnets and not steel?
questions.answer: Soft iron has high permeability (concentrates field) and low retentivity (loses magnetism quickly when current off). Steel has high retentivity → remains magnetised → unsuitable for temporary magnets.

questions.question: Explain why AC is preferred over DC for long-distance power transmission.
questions.answer: AC can be stepped up/down using transformers (high voltage low current → reduced I²R loss). DC cannot be transformed easily. AC transmission more efficient and economical.

questions.question: Describe the pattern of magnetic field lines around a bar magnet. How do they differ from current-carrying solenoid?
questions.answer: Closed loops from N to S externally, S to N internally. Crowded at poles (strong field). Solenoid similar but uniform inside, weaker outside. Both act as bar magnets.

questions.question: Explain the role of carbon brushes and slip rings in an AC generator.
questions.answer: Slip rings maintain contact with rotating coil ends, allowing current collection without twisting wires. Carbon brushes press against rings to transfer induced AC to external circuit smoothly.

questions.question: State the factors affecting magnetic field strength at a point due to a long straight current-carrying conductor.
questions.answer: B = μ₀ I / (2π r). Depends on: Current I (direct), distance r (inverse), permeability μ₀ of medium. Independent of conductor material.

questions.question: Explain how electromagnetic induction is used in induction cooktops or wireless charging. Mention the underlying principle.
questions.answer: Alternating current in coil produces changing magnetic field → induces eddy currents in vessel/base → heating (cooktop) or in receiver coil → charging. Based on Faraday's law and Lenz's law.
